# [Colorado State University](https://www.colostate.edu)

## [SOURCE](https://source.colostate.edu/)

[navigation menu](#)

# Interdisciplinary team uses AI powered tools for protein engineering in medicine and beyond

By [Josh Rhoten](https://source.colostate.edu/author/c824188513/)

# AI-powered research

###### Interdisciplinary team uses AI-powered tools for protein engineering in medicine and beyond

By Josh Rhoten  
Published April 10, 2025

Interdisciplinary researchers at Colorado State University are using AI powered tools to rapidly re-engineer and discover new proteins – a fundamental building block of all life as we know it. Their work is part of a growing movement to apply artificial intelligence techniques to better understand how living cells operate, with broad applications in medicine, including in the battle against infectious diseases.

Proteins are microscopic molecules that dictate everything from the behavior of viruses and bacteria to the regular operation of the human body. These tiny catalysts are made of a unique string of amino acids that determine both their 3D structure and function. Researchers have worked for decades to predict how proteins fold into these unique shapes to then build muscle or regulate hormones, among many other key processes.

These complex shapes can also inform the ways antibodies spot and react to diseases attacking our immune systems. With that in mind, researchers have been working to find or engineer proteins that can then be used as powerful tools in treatments such as drug development or to improve our basic understanding of cell function.

[Christopher Snow](https://www.engr.colostate.edu/cbe/people/chris-snow/), a professor in the [Department of Chemical and Biological Engineering](https://www.engr.colostate.edu/cbe/), said discovering and then outlining a protein’s shape was a time intensive process in the past. However, AI tools now allow researchers to quickly identify and predict how proteins may interact and specifically link together like a lock and key with other molecules for a given purpose or need.

New AI tools can also help guide researchers to genetically design proteins that may not even exist in nature but would be useful, said Snow.

“I have always tried to embrace the latest technology in my lab, and AI is clearly changing the field of biomolecular engineering,” he said. “The combination of tools available to us presents an exciting opportunity to make the research and identification processes more effective.”

Snow said Google DeepMind’s AlphaFold2 first broke barriers in 2020 and is still the most popular and effective AI tool for protein structure prediction today. Depending on the size of the target, high-confidence models can be made in minutes with the system about a given protein’s structure when it folds. Since then, third generation methods, such as AlphaFold3 and its competitors, have emerged and extend the predictions to non-protein components like small molecules and nucleic acids. In many cases, the predictions are good enough to enable understanding without time-consuming real-life experimentation.

“That capability was a holy grail for the field for decades. Since the introduction of this neural network, we can now rapidly predict shapes with an accuracy that is competitive with what may come out of long, physical experimentation,” Snow said. “We used to have to cut and copy fragments of protein antibodies together, for example, to see if they were compatible and or useful for a given purpose we were pursuing. That worked OK, but machine learning has improved that process dramatically.”

Snow also pointed to two other AI tools his team was leveraging for this work. ProteinMPNN – a neural network developed at the University of Washington – looks at a given protein’s 3D structure and then predicts optimized amino acid sequences for that target structure. Those tweaks could make the proteins more stable or improve their overall function compared to naturally occurring versions.

Snow also uses RFdiffusion to invent totally new protein backbone structures. The system develops these potential leads for researchers to consider in a similar way to AI art generation software. Those AI systems are trained to remove random noise that has been added to existing images and, ultimately, they can then generate altogether new images starting from pure noise. Here the system considers what it knows about the shapes of existing 3D protein structures to see if new workable combinations can be found from the endless possible combination of shapes available. This process generates avenues for researchers to further explore in pursuit of vaccines and other medical treatments.

Snow said his team uses these tools and others in their daily work.

“These AI tools are not perfect, but their makers knew that and included aspects in their code that point out where the system is more confident and less confident in its findings,” he said. “The applications are quite broad and have really accelerated our ability to now design needed biomolecules, biomaterials, and devices.”

## Interdisciplinary biomolecular engineering research at Colorado State

Snow has been working with these AI tools in partnership with professors [Brian Geiss](https://vetmedbiosci.colostate.edu/directory/member/?id=brian-geiss-3203), from the Department of Microbiology, Immunology, and Pathology, and [Tim Stasevich](https://stasevichlab.colostate.edu/), from the Department of Biochemistry and Molecular Biology. All three faculty also hold appointments in the [School of Biomedical Engineering at CSU](https://www.engr.colostate.edu/sbme/). Together, the group is known as TagTeam on campus and includes former Postdoctoral Researcher Ning Zhao, who is now a faculty member at the University of Colorado Anschutz.

While they agree that biomolecular engineering has broad applications even beyond medicine, the team is particularly interested in studying the inner workings of the cell with this technology.

“I have always tried to embrace the latest technology in my lab, and AI is clearly changing the field of biomolecular engineering. The combination of tools available to us presents an exciting opportunity to make the research and identification processes more effective.”

Christopher Snow

Professor, Chemical and Biological Engineering

Scientists currently use a method where they attach a second glowing protein to an existing protein to watch it move inside a cell and study its location and interactions. But that approach might ultimately change how the protein behaves. Instead, the CSU team is working to engineer special proteins that can be tracked as they find and stick to natural proteins inside cells without needing those permanent glowing tags, Geiss said.

“We have traditionally had to add big fluorescent proteins to host or viral proteins in order to see the processes we are interested in, which is like hooking a U-Haul trailer to a race car and expecting the race car to be as fast as normal,” Geiss said. “Being able to specifically engineer antibodies and novel proteins that then recognize natural proteins in living cells is a dream come true for cell biologists and virologists, since it lets us see how they behave in a much more natural setting.”

The focus of this collaborative work and technology has been studying how antibody proteins can recognize other proteins – especially viral proteins – through a part of their 3D structures called epitopes. An epitope is a small part of a protein that enables the larger immune system to recognize the threat as it battles infection.

Antibodies produced by our immune systems can recognize specific epitopes to protect against disease, and they have long been used as molecular magnets to detect proteins in cells. The TagTeam has been using their expertise to now design antibodies that can detect viral proteins in living cells. This allows researchers to view how these key proteins move and interact in cells in real time, providing much richer information about these complicated systems.

AI tools like AlphaFold2 are used to quickly predict where proteins could potentially attach. For instance, the team successfully used the program to predict the binding site of a new COVID-19 antibody. Stasevich said they confirmed those findings with lab experiments, and the project was published in two manuscripts – in eLife in January 2025 and the other still in preprint form.

“The more you know about where and when virus proteins go in a cell and when the virus is replicating, the more information you have to develop interventions and therapeutic applications like vaccines,” Stasevich said. “Our approach lets us see the inner workings of the cell in great resolution and assists in understanding how antibodies bind their targets.”

The Revolution in Drug Discovery: How AI-Powered Protein Prediction is Transforming Medicine
============================================================================================

[![Image 3: Oluwafemidiakhoa](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fill:32:32/1*XCwRBfNOEJ4Ux2hk88Ndgw.jpeg)](https://medium.com/@oluwafemidiakhoa?source=post_page---byline--b12617c3aa0a---------------------------------------)

![Image 4](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:700/1*B-uus4JJAc2S_oI0cBqiSw.png)

In recent years, the field of drug discovery has witnessed a groundbreaking transformation. Central to this revolution is the advent of AI-powered protein prediction technology, which promises to accelerate the identification and development of new drug candidates. This article delves into the intricacies of this technology, its impact on drug discovery, and the potential it holds for future medical advancements.

Proteins are essential molecules in biological systems, serving as enzymes, receptors, and structural components, among other roles. The three-dimensional structure of a protein dictates its function and its interactions with other molecules. Historically, determining these structures has been a labor-intensive and expensive process, relying on methods such as X-ray crystallography and cryo-electron microscopy. These traditional techniques, while powerful, are limited by their complexity and the time required to obtain high-resolution images of protein structures.

Artificial intelligence, particularly through advancements made by DeepMind’s AlphaFold, has revolutionized our ability to predict protein structures accurately. AlphaFold2 has demonstrated an unprecedented level of accuracy, often comparable to experimental methods, thus providing researchers with detailed structural insights without the need for extensive laboratory work. This breakthrough has significant implications for drug discovery, enabling scientists to identify potential drug targets more quickly and design molecules that can precisely interact with these targets.

The application of AI in protein prediction not only speeds up the drug discovery process but also enhances the precision and efficiency of drug design. By leveraging vast amounts of sequence data and sophisticated algorithms, AI can predict how proteins fold and function, providing a robust foundation for developing new therapeutics. As we explore the impact of this technology on drug discovery, we will examine its historical context, current applications, and prospects, illustrating how AI is transforming medicine and offering new hope for treating a wide range of diseases.

In the following sections, we will delve deeper into the significance of protein structures, the evolution of prediction technologies, the specifics of AlphaFold’s impact, and the broader implications for drug discovery and beyond.

The Importance of Protein Structures in Drug Discovery
======================================================

![Image 5](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:700/1*ZOiQ8aoEPYze9dlYtOqN9Q.png)

Proteins are the fundamental building blocks of life, performing a myriad of functions within living organisms. They act as enzymes, catalysts that speed up biochemical reactions, and as structural components, providing support and shape to cells and tissues. Proteins also function as signaling molecules, allowing cells to communicate with each other, and as transporters, moving substances across cellular membranes. The specific function of a protein is determined by its three-dimensional structure, which is, in turn, dictated by the sequence of amino acids that make up the protein.

Understanding the three-dimensional structure of proteins is crucial in drug discovery for several reasons. Firstly, the shape of a protein determines how it interacts with other molecules. For a drug to be effective, it must bind to its target protein with high specificity and affinity. This binding often occurs at specific sites on the protein’s surface, known as active sites or binding pockets. Knowing the precise structure of a protein enables researchers to design drugs that fit these sites perfectly, much like a key fit into a lock. This approach, known as structure-based drug design, has been instrumental in developing many successful therapeutics.

Historically, determining the structure of a protein has been a challenging and time-consuming task. Techniques such as X-ray crystallography and cryo-electron microscopy have been the gold standards in structural biology. X-ray crystallography involves crystallizing the protein and then bombarding it with X-rays to produce a diffraction pattern, which can be analyzed to determine the protein’s structure. Cryo-electron microscopy, on the other hand, involves flash-freezing the protein and then imaging it with an electron microscope. While these techniques have provided detailed insights into protein structures, they are not without limitations.

X-ray crystallography requires the protein to be in a crystalline form, which is not always feasible for all proteins, especially those that are large or membrane bound. Additionally, the crystallization process itself can alter the natural structure of the protein. Cryo-electron microscopy, although powerful, often produces lower resolution images compared to X-ray crystallography, and the preparation of samples can be technically demanding.

The advent of AI-powered protein prediction technology has the potential to overcome these limitations. By leveraging vast amounts of sequence data and sophisticated algorithms, AI can predict the three-dimensional structure of a protein from its amino acid sequence alone. This capability represents a significant advancement in the field of structural biology and has far-reaching implications for drug discovery.

One of the most notable AI-powered tools in this domain is AlphaFold, developed by DeepMind. AlphaFold uses deep learning algorithms to predict protein structures with remarkable accuracy. In the recent Critical Assessment of protein Structure Prediction (CASP) competition, AlphaFold achieved a median accuracy comparable to experimental methods for a wide range of proteins, demonstrating its potential to revolutionize the field​.

AI-powered protein prediction not only accelerates the process of determining protein structures but also opens new possibilities for identifying drug targets. By providing detailed structural insights, AI enables researchers to design drugs that can precisely interact with their target proteins, increasing the likelihood of developing effective therapies with fewer side effects.

In conclusion, the ability to accurately predict protein structures is a game-changer in drug discovery. It enhances our understanding of protein function and interactions, facilitates the design of targeted therapies, and accelerates the overall drug development process. As AI technology continues to advance, it promises to unlock new opportunities in biomedical research and improve our ability to treat a wide range of diseases.

The Evolution of Protein Prediction Technology
==============================================

![Image 6](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:700/1*wF5yJUjsvhN80YBTmyAclA.png)

The quest to predict protein structures dates back several decades and has been marked by numerous breakthroughs and incremental advancements. The challenge lies in the sheer complexity of proteins: each protein is composed of a long chain of amino acids that can fold into an astronomical number of shapes. Predicting the correct three-dimensional structure from the amino acid sequence alone was considered one of the most formidable challenges in computational biology.

In the 1970s, Christian Anfinsen’s Nobel Prize-winning work demonstrated that the amino acid sequence of a protein determines its structure, laying the foundation for the field of protein folding. However, the actual prediction of protein structures remained elusive due to the vast computational resources required and the complexity of the folding process​ ​.

The field saw considerable progress with the establishment of the Critical Assessment of protein Structure Prediction (CASP) in 1994. CASP is a biennial competition that provides a platform for scientists to test their protein structure prediction methods against a set of benchmark proteins whose structures are known but not yet published. CASP has played a crucial role in driving innovation and setting benchmarks for accuracy in protein prediction methods.

Early computational methods relied heavily on physics-based approaches and homology modeling. Physics-based approaches, such as molecular dynamics simulations, attempt to model the physical forces and interactions that drive protein folding. While these methods can provide detailed insights, they are computationally intensive and often impractical for large-scale predictions. Homology modeling, on the other hand, predicts the structure of a protein based on its similarity to known protein structures. This method works well when homologous structures are available but struggles with novel proteins that lack close relatives in the structural database.

The advent of machine learning and, more specifically, deep learning, marked a turning point in the field. Deep learning algorithms excel at identifying patterns in large datasets, making them well-suited for the complex task of protein structure prediction. The introduction of AlphaFold by DeepMind in 2018 was a watershed moment. AlphaFold utilized deep learning techniques to predict protein structures with unprecedented accuracy, outperforming all other methods in the CASP13 competition​​.

AlphaFold’s success can be attributed to its innovative use of deep neural networks. The algorithm was trained on a large dataset of known protein structures and sequence alignments, allowing it to learn the intricate relationships between amino acid sequences and their corresponding 3D structures. By leveraging this knowledge, AlphaFold could predict the distances and angles between amino acid pairs, constructing highly accurate 3D models of proteins.

The release of AlphaFold2 in 2020 further cemented DeepMind’s dominance in the field. AlphaFold2 introduced several improvements over its predecessor, including a new architecture that combines convolutional neural networks with attention mechanisms. This hybrid approach allows AlphaFold2 to capture both local and global structural features of proteins, resulting in even higher accuracy. In the CASP14 competition, AlphaFold2 achieved a median accuracy comparable to experimental methods for a wide range of proteins, a feat that was previously considered unattainable​.

The impact of AlphaFold extends beyond its performance in competitions. The open-source release of AlphaFold2’s code and predicted structures for all human proteins has democratized access to high-quality structural data. Researchers worldwide can now leverage these predictions to accelerate their own studies, whether in basic biology, drug discovery, or biotechnology.

The success of AlphaFold has also spurred the development of other AI-driven protein prediction tools. Competing algorithms, such as RoseTTAFold from the University of Washington and ESMFold from Facebook AI Research, have emerged, each offering unique strengths and contributing to a rapidly advancing field. These tools are collectively pushing the boundaries of what is possible in protein structure prediction, bringing us closer to a comprehensive understanding of the protein universe.

In conclusion, the evolution of protein prediction technology has been a journey of overcoming immense computational and scientific challenges. From the early days of physics-based models to the current era of deep learning, each advancement has brought us closer to the goal of accurately predicting protein structures. The breakthroughs achieved by AlphaFold and similar technologies are not only transforming drug discovery but also opening new frontiers in our understanding of biology. As these tools continue to evolve, they hold the promise of revolutionizing medicine and improving human health on a global scale.

AlphaFold and Its Impact
========================

![Image 7](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:700/1*F6XTHPkEzL0BDtgwiFpcEg.png)

The release of AlphaFold by DeepMind marked a watershed moment in the field of protein structure prediction, propelling it from a challenging scientific problem to a practical tool with far-reaching implications for drug discovery and beyond. AlphaFold’s remarkable accuracy in predicting protein structures has revolutionized our understanding of biology and opened new avenues for therapeutic development.

The Science Behind AlphaFold
----------------------------

AlphaFold’s success lies in its innovative use of deep learning techniques. The algorithm employs a deep neural network trained on a vast dataset of known protein structures and sequence alignments. The core idea is to predict the distances and angles between pairs of amino acids within a protein sequence, thereby determining the overall 3D structure. AlphaFold’s architecture integrates multiple sources of information, including evolutionary relationships, co-evolutionary data, and physical constraints of protein folding.

One of the key breakthroughs of AlphaFold is its ability to predict the accuracy of its own predictions. This self-assessment capability allows researchers to gauge the reliability of the predicted structures and focus on the most confident results. In the CASP13 competition held in 2018, AlphaFold outperformed all other methods, achieving a median Global Distance Test (GDT) score that was significantly higher than its competitors. This was a clear demonstration of the potential of deep learning in structural biology​ ​.

AlphaFold2: Raising the Bar
---------------------------

Building on the success of the original AlphaFold, DeepMind introduced AlphaFold2 in 2020. AlphaFold2 incorporated several advancements, including a new neural network architecture that combined convolutional networks with attention mechanisms. This hybrid approach enabled the model to capture both local and global structural features, significantly improving its accuracy.

In the CASP14 competition, AlphaFold2 achieved a median GDT score of 92.4, approaching the accuracy of experimental methods such as X-ray crystallography and cryo-electron microscopy. This level of precision was previously thought to be unattainable through computational methods alone. AlphaFold2’s predictions were not only accurate but also covered a wide range of proteins, including those with complex and previously unsolved structures​ .

Real-World Applications
-----------------------

The implications of AlphaFold’s breakthroughs extend far beyond academic competitions. The ability to accurately predict protein structures has profound implications for drug discovery. Pharmaceutical companies can now use AlphaFold’s predictions to identify new drug targets, design molecules that interact with these targets, and optimize drug candidates for better efficacy and safety.

One notable application of AlphaFold’s technology is in the fight against COVID-19. Early in the pandemic, researchers used AlphaFold to predict the structures of several proteins of the SARS-CoV-2 virus. These predictions provided valuable insights into the virus’s biology and aided in the development of vaccines and antiviral drugs. The rapid and accurate predictions made by AlphaFold demonstrated its potential to respond to emerging health crises and accelerate the development of therapeutic interventions​.

Democratizing Protein Structure Data
------------------------------------

A significant milestone in the impact of AlphaFold was the open-source release of its code and the publication of predicted structures for all proteins encoded by the human genome. This massive dataset, made freely available to the scientific community, has democratized access to high-quality structural data. Researchers around the world can now leverage these predictions to advance their own studies, fostering a collaborative and inclusive approach to scientific discovery.

The availability of AlphaFold’s predictions has already led to several breakthroughs in various fields. In structural biology, researchers have used AlphaFold’s models to study previously intractable proteins, uncovering new insights into their functions and interactions. In biotechnology, the structural data provided by AlphaFold is being used to engineer proteins with novel properties, paving the way for innovative applications in medicine, agriculture, and industry​ .

Beyond AlphaFold: The Future of Protein Prediction
--------------------------------------------------

The success of AlphaFold has inspired the development of other AI-driven protein prediction tools. Competing algorithms like RoseTTAFold from the University of Washington and ESMFold from Facebook AI Research have emerged, each contributing unique strengths to the field. These tools are collectively pushing the boundaries of what is possible in protein structure prediction, bringing us closer to a comprehensive understanding of the protein universe.

Looking ahead, the future of protein prediction lies in integrating these computational models with experimental data to achieve even greater accuracy and coverage. Advances in high-throughput experimental techniques, such as cryo-electron microscopy and mass spectrometry, will provide valuable data to further refine and validate AI predictions. Additionally, ongoing improvements in computational power and algorithm design will enable the prediction of increasingly complex protein structures and their dynamic behaviors​ ([MDPI](https://www.mdpi.com/2218-273X/14/3/339))​​ ([Nature](https://www.nature.com/articles/d42473-021-00369-z))​.

AlphaFold’s impact on the field of protein structure prediction cannot be overstated. By achieving a level of accuracy comparable to experimental methods, AlphaFold has revolutionized our ability to understand and manipulate the fundamental building blocks of life. Its applications in drug discovery, biotechnology, and beyond are already transforming the landscape of biomedical research, offering new hope for the development of effective therapies.

As we continue to explore the potential of AI-driven protein prediction, we are only at the beginning of a new era in structural biology. The breakthroughs achieved by AlphaFold, and similar technologies are opening new frontiers in science and medicine, promising to improve human health and well-being on a global scale.

AI and Drug Discovery: A Perfect Match
======================================

![Image 8](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:700/1*BDrfZJ2MFDSvdnJSohuk-g.png)

Artificial intelligence (AI) has revolutionized many industries, and its integration into drug discovery processes is proving to be a game-changer. By leveraging the power of machine learning and vast datasets, AI can significantly accelerate the identification and optimization of drug candidates, making the entire process more efficient and cost-effective. This chapter explores how AI is used in various stages of drug discovery, highlighting its impact and potential.

Virtual Screening
-----------------

One of the critical applications of AI in drug discovery is virtual screening. This process involves using computational models to evaluate large libraries of chemical compounds and identify those most likely to bind to a target protein. Traditional methods of virtual screening often require significant computational resources and time. AI enhances this process by rapidly analyzing vast amounts of data and identifying promising candidates with high accuracy.

Machine learning models, trained on extensive datasets of known protein-ligand interactions, can predict the binding affinity of new compounds. These predictions help prioritize compounds for further experimental testing, significantly reducing the time and cost associated with drug discovery. AI-driven virtual screening has been successfully applied in various therapeutic areas, including oncology, infectious diseases, and neurology​ ​.

Optimization of Drug Candidates
-------------------------------

Once potential drug candidates are identified, AI can further optimize them by predicting how modifications to their chemical structures will affect their binding affinity, selectivity, and pharmacokinetic properties. Machine learning algorithms, such as generative adversarial networks (GANs) and reinforcement learning, are particularly effective in this context.

For instance, GANs can generate new chemical structures with desired properties by learning from existing compounds. These AI-generated compounds can then be synthesized and tested experimentally. Reinforcement learning, on the other hand, can iteratively improve drug candidates by exploring a vast chemical space and optimizing specific objectives, such as potency and selectivity. This approach allows researchers to fine-tune drug candidates more efficiently, increasing the likelihood of success in preclinical and clinical trials​ ​.

Predicting Protein-Ligand Interactions
--------------------------------------

AI is also transforming our understanding of protein-ligand interactions. Advanced algorithms can model the dynamic interactions between proteins and small molecules, providing insights into binding mechanisms and potential off-target effects. This level of detail is crucial for designing drugs with high specificity and minimal side effects.

Recent advancements in deep learning have led to the development of models that can predict the binding pose and affinity of ligands to their target proteins with remarkable accuracy. These models are trained on large datasets of experimental binding data and can generalize to novel protein-ligand pairs. By accurately predicting how a drug candidate interacts with its target, AI helps identify the most promising compounds and streamline the drug development process​ ​.

Reducing Time and Cost
----------------------

The traditional drug discovery process is notoriously time-consuming and expensive, often taking over a decade and billions of dollars to bring a new drug to market. AI addresses these challenges by accelerating various stages of the process, from target identification to lead optimization and preclinical testing.

AI-driven tools can quickly analyze large datasets, identify patterns, and generate hypotheses, allowing researchers to make data-driven decisions. This efficiency reduces the need for extensive experimental testing and enables faster iterations, shortening the drug development timeline. Additionally, by predicting potential failures early in the process, AI helps avoid costly late-stage failures and improves the overall success rate of drug development​ ​.

Case Studies
------------

Several case studies highlight the practical benefits of AI-driven drug discovery. For example, during the COVID-19 pandemic, AI played a crucial role in identifying potential antiviral compounds and accelerating the development of vaccines. AI models predicted the structures of viral proteins, facilitating the design of inhibitors and informing vaccine development efforts. These AI-driven insights contributed to the rapid response to the pandemic, demonstrating the technology’s potential to address urgent public health challenges​.

Another notable example is the use of AI in oncology. Pharmaceutical companies have leveraged AI to identify new cancer targets, design novel therapeutics, and optimize existing treatments. AI models have successfully predicted the efficacy of drug combinations and personalized treatment regimens based on patient-specific data, leading to improved outcomes in clinical trials and personalized medicine​.

The integration of AI into drug discovery represents a perfect match, combining the strengths of computational power and data analysis with the expertise of biomedical research. AI enhances every stage of the drug discovery process, from virtual screening to lead optimization and beyond. By reducing time and cost, improving accuracy, and increasing the likelihood of success, AI is transforming the landscape of drug development and offering new hope for the treatment of various diseases. As AI technology continues to advance, its impact on drug discovery will only grow, paving the way for more efficient and effective therapeutics in the future.

Overcoming Challenges and Limitations
=====================================

![Image 9](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:649/1*-Qs-2NoiNaqdyuk8I1sYnQ.png)

Despite the impressive advancements in AI-powered protein prediction, several challenges and limitations still need to be addressed to fully harness its potential in drug discovery. This chapter explores some of these hurdles and the ongoing efforts to overcome them.

Accuracy and Reliability
------------------------

While AI models like AlphaFold2 have achieved remarkable accuracy, they are fallible. Some protein structures, particularly those with high flexibility or those that form complexes with other molecules, remain challenging to predict accurately. The dynamic nature of proteins means that they can adopt multiple conf

# ROOM LOCATION: Republic Ballroom A

# AI/ML FOR DRUG DESIGN & OPTIMIZATION

Chairperson's Remarks

Patrick Riley, PhD, Senior Vice President, Artificial Intelligence, Computation Department, Relay Therapeutics, Inc.

Automated Chemical Design in Drug Discovery

![](/images/default-source/speakerphotos/riley_patrick-jpg_0703b25a-a0dd-424d-8544-0bea1709cb72.tmb-.jpg?Status=Master&sfvrsn=acdef065_1)
Patrick Riley, PhD, Senior Vice President, Artificial Intelligence, Computation Department, Relay Therapeutics, Inc.

![](/images/default-source/speakerphotos/riley_patrick-jpg_0703b25a-a0dd-424d-8544-0bea1709cb72.tmb-.jpg?Status=Master&sfvrsn=acdef065_1)

I’ll cover a framework we call ACD (Automated Chemical Design) Levels for describing the level of autonomy for AI-powered systems that design molecules. This framework allows relevant distinctions to be drawn and can help teams better understand the claimed capabilities of a system and ask more insightful questions to understand their function. Using these definitions, we will discuss the challenges and opportunity for AI-powered design of molecules in drug discovery.

Combining Generative AI Models and Reinforcement Learning for *de novo* Drug Discovery

![](/images/default-source/speakerphotos/srinivasan_parthiban-jpg_3d6cccb6-a3ed-483f-9d2d-8369ec2a2c2b.tmb-.jpg?Status=Master&sfvrsn=1d8184a2_1)
Parthiban Srinivasan, PhD, Professor, Data Science and Engineering, Indian Institute of Science Education and Research

![](/images/default-source/speakerphotos/srinivasan_parthiban-jpg_3d6cccb6-a3ed-483f-9d2d-8369ec2a2c2b.tmb-.jpg?Status=Master&sfvrsn=1d8184a2_1)

Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) and Reinforcement Learning (RL) have been successfully applied for *de novo* drug design. Multiple such frameworks have been explored and shared by many AI researchers. We review these models and discuss the features of these techniques in terms of generating novel molecules with desired properties.

![](https://ciiprodexhpht.blob.core.windows.net/company-photos/dot222600-causaly-companyLogo-WebReady.png)

![](https://ciiprodexhpht.blob.core.windows.net/speaker-photos/dot222600-causaly-Richard-Harrison-30-Minute-Presentation.jpg)
Richard Harrison, Chief Scientist, Causaly

![](https://ciiprodexhpht.blob.core.windows.net/speaker-photos/dot222600-causaly-Richard-Harrison-30-Minute-Presentation.jpg)

It is well known that 90% of drugs entering clinical trials fail to make it to market. Furthermore, a failure to link the target to a disease in preclinical research is the major reason for these failures. Using AI to machine-read and comprehend all scientific literature, we will demonstrate how Causaly Cloud can uncover hidden mediators for several diseases, ensuring the identification of candidates with the greatest chance of success.

Grand Opening Coffee Break in the Exhibit Hall with Poster Viewing (Grand Ballroom)9:35 am

CACHE: An Experimental Platform to Benchmark and Reveal the Future of Virtual Screening Methods

![](/images/default-source/speakerphotos/schapira_matthieu-png_9e082fa2-09ea-4dec-b787-f6f04f511fbf.tmb-.png?Status=Master&sfvrsn=61e262f1_7)
Matthieu Schapira, PhD, Principal Investigator, Structural Genomics Consortium

![](/images/default-source/speakerphotos/schapira_matthieu-png_9e082fa2-09ea-4dec-b787-f6f04f511fbf.tmb-.png?Status=Master&sfvrsn=61e262f1_7)

The Critical Assessment of Computational Hit-Finding Experiments (CACHE) is an international competition where computational chemists and AI experts around the world submit their predicted compounds which are then procured and rigorously tested experimentally at CACHE against a pre-defined target. The first two challenges, to find hits for the Parkinson’s disease target LRRK2 and the SARS-CoV-2 helicase NSP13 are ongoing. ~50% of participants combined physics-based and deep-learning techniques. Do these hybrid methods already out-perform more traditional approaches?

Solubility Prediction by Deep Learning of Quantum Information Embedded in a Novel Molecular Representation

![](/images/default-source/speakerphotos/li_tonglei-jpg_553364a8-8163-489f-a4f0-b7bac5ef16f0.tmb-.jpg?Status=Master&sfvrsn=63502cc7_1)
Tonglei Li, PhD, Allen Chao Chair & Professor, Industrial & Physical Pharmacy, Purdue University

![](/images/default-source/speakerphotos/li_tonglei-jpg_553364a8-8163-489f-a4f0-b7bac5ef16f0.tmb-.jpg?Status=Master&sfvrsn=63502cc7_1)

Solubility can be a challenging, time-consuming, material intensive property to measure correctly. To accurately predict solubility values of drug-like compounds, we have recently developed a molecular featurization scheme – MEMS, or Manifold Embedding of Molecular Surface – based on electronic properties on the surface of a molecule. Deep learning methods were further developed. Our effort shows promise in solubility prediction with an accuracy outperforming most of the reported models.

PANEL DISCUSSION: Key Learnings from AI-Driven Early Drug Discovery

Patrick Riley, PhD, Senior Vice President, Artificial Intelligence, Computation Department, Relay Therapeutics, Inc.

Anthony Bradley, PhD, Director of Design Development, Exscientia Ltd.

Jörg Wegner, PhD, Associate Scientific Director, In Silico Discovery and External Innovation, Janssen Research & Development, LLC

![](https://ciiprodexhpht.blob.core.windows.net/company-photos/dot222600-eurofins-companyLogo-WebReady.png)

![](https://ciiprodexhpht.blob.core.windows.net/speaker-photos/dot222600-eurofins-Carleton-Sage-15-Minute-Presentation.jpg)
Carleton Sage, PhD, Vice President, Computational Sciences, Eurofins Discovery

![](https://ciiprodexhpht.blob.core.windows.net/speaker-photos/dot222600-eurofins-Carleton-Sage-15-Minute-Presentation.jpg)

Based on predictive crystal structures made available by Google’s DeepMind AlphaFold project, Eurofins Discovery will explore the potential of computational chemistry alongside HTS procedures to present examples of diverse and complementary approaches to hit discovery using two ongoing GPCR discovery projects.  We will discuss target selection rationale in a virtual screening approach and a parallel effort of an HTS campaign to address the challenges of hit confirmation and validation.

Enjoy Lunch on Your Own12:10 pm

# UNDERSTANDING THE CAVEATS OF AI PREDICTIONS

Chairperson's Remarks

Anthony Bradley, PhD, Director of Design Development, Exscientia Ltd.

Patient-first AI​: Exscientia’s Approach

![](/images/default-source/speakerphotos/bradley_anthony-png_a5dd95b0-d1b4-4dd2-bde5-215d5b552aa1.tmb-.png?Status=Master&sfvrsn=5569d8c3_0)
Anthony Bradley, PhD, Director of Design Development, Exscientia Ltd.

![](/images/default-source/speakerphotos/bradley_anthony-png_a5dd95b0-d1b4-4dd2-bde5-215d5b552aa1.tmb-.png?Status=Master&sfvrsn=5569d8c3_0)

Exscientia’s patient-first approach to drug discovery has produced industry-leading productivity. Structural information is crucial across this process – from target ID, hit generation, and compound optimisation. The revolution in availability of computational and experimental structural information further drives Exscientia's efficiency and predictive quality. In this talk, we outline how we leverage the power of physics and informatics in our AI algorithms. We exemplify this through high-throughput structural target assessment, delivery of novel and class-leading potential anti-viral programme for SARS-CoV-2, and in delivering automated and *de novo* potent hits in the first cycle of design for a kinase.

Opportunities and Challenges on “Which Compounds to Make” and “How to Make Them” – A Scalability Perspective

![](/images/default-source/speakerphotos/wegner_jorg-jpeg_c8aa820b-22ff-427d-8164-788da74c646c.tmb-.jpeg?Status=Master&sfvrsn=9255c01a_1)
Jörg Wegner, PhD, Associate Scientific Director, In Silico Discovery and External Innovation, Janssen Research & Development, LLC

![](/images/default-source/speakerphotos/wegner_jorg-jpeg_c8aa820b-22ff-427d-8164-788da74c646c.tmb-.jpeg?Status=Master&sfvrsn=9255c01a_1)

We will showcase how data analytics/AI is being used in business processes and how this improves decision-making for drug design teams. We will present retro- and prospective studies to highlight the value proposition of the innovation integration of novel science and technology into business processes. A key differentiator for enabling analytics/AI is to decide which science and technology contributes to the business and only lift those to enterprise level.

![](https://ciiprodexhpht.blob.core.windows.net/company-photos/dot222600-charles-river-companyLogo-WebReady.png)

![](https://ciiprodexhpht.blob.core.windows.net/speaker-photos/dot222600-charles-river-Ronald-Dorenbos-30-Minute-Presentation.jfif)
Ronald Dorenbos, Executive Director Business Development, Logica

![](https://ciiprodexhpht.blob.core.windows.net/speaker-photos/dot222600-charles-river-Ronald-Dorenbos-30-Minute-Presentation.jfif)

Charles River Laboratories and Valo Health have launched Logica, an artificial intelligence (AI) powered drug solution that directly translates clients’ biological insights into optimized preclinical assets. Logica leverages Valo’s AI-powered Opal Computational Platform and Charles River’s leading preclinical expertise, providing clients with transformed drug discovery with a single integrated offering seamlessly translating targets to candidate nomination.

Refreshment Break in the Exhibit Hall with Poster Viewing (Grand Ballroom)3:00 pm

How AI Is Accelerating Drug Discovery

![](/images/default-source/speakerphotos/kamya_petrina-jpg_66e40834-9c17-48ad-9514-e05c4645e857.tmb-.jpg?Status=Master&sfvrsn=1af340ce_1)
Petrina Kamya, PhD, Head of AI Platforms, Department of Business Development, Insilico Medicine

![](/images/default-source/speakerphotos/kamya_petrina-jpg_66e40834-9c17-48ad-9514-e05c4645e857.tmb-.jpg?Status=Master&sfvrsn=1af340ce_1)

![](/images/default-source/speakerphotos/zhavoronkov_alex-png_055807e4-12f8-4ce9-87ee-e3ad534a0c4c.tmb-.png?Status=Master&sfvrsn=f4a04b41_11)
Alex Zhavoronkov, PhD, Founder & CEO, Insilico Medicine

![](/images/default-source/speakerphotos/zhavoronkov_alex-png_055807e4-12f8-4ce9-87ee-e3ad534a0c4c.tmb-.png?Status=Master&sfvrsn=f4a04b41_11)

Bringing just one drug to market through traditional research and discovery is a decade-long process that costs over $2 billion and the vast majority of drugs in development fail. I will discuss how artificial intelligence is ushering in a new era of accelerated drug discovery through a revolutionary machine learning platform that incorporates novel target discovery, novel small molecule design, and clinical trial prediction. *In silico* medicine is a global pioneer in end-to-end AI-driven drug discovery with the first AI-discovered and AI-designed drug for idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis currently in Stage 1 clinical trials and 7 preclinical candidates developed.

The Potential Dark Side of Generative AI for Drug Discovery

![](/images/default-source/speakerphotos/ekins_sean-jpg_36832275-d12d-487e-b8ac-51ab5f022b27.tmb-.jpg?Status=Master&sfvrsn=b6ae6a2c_0)
Sean Ekins, PhD, Founder & CEO, Collaborations Pharmaceuticals, Inc.

![](/images/default-source/speakerphotos/ekins_sean-jpg_36832275-d12d-487e-b8ac-51ab5f022b27.tmb-.jpg?Status=Master&sfvrsn=b6ae6a2c_0)

Our recent work describing the use of generative approaches to rapidly develop thousands of virtual molecules, including the nerve agent VX, has drawn wide attention from the global media. We will address some of the many AI-related ethical questions we have faced since, including whether we should have published the work in the first place and our motivations behind it. In the process we will propose mechanisms whereby such software, data and models can be securely shared.

Each discussion will be led by a facilitator who keeps the discussion on track and the group engaged. To get the most out of this format, please come prepared to share examples from your work, be a part of a collective, problem-solving session, and participate in active idea sharing. # AI FOR PROTEIN THERAPEUTICS

Chairperson's Remarks

Ryan Henrici, MD, PhD, Director of Translational Research, BigHat Biosciences

Designing Therapeutic Antibodies with Synthetic Biology and Machine Learning

![](/images/default-source/speakerphotos/henrici_ryan-jpg_c7e86d42-2ce9-4e6b-82ff-d3575ddab55a.tmb-.jpg?Status=Master&sfvrsn=38012373_1)
Ryan Henrici, MD, PhD, Director of Translational Research, BigHat Biosciences

![](/images/default-source/speakerphotos/henrici_ryan-jpg_c7e86d42-2ce9-4e6b-82ff-d3575ddab55a.tmb-.jpg?Status=Master&sfvrsn=38012373_1)

BigHat Biosciences is designing safer, more effective antibody therapies for patients using machine learning and synthetic biology. Machine learning guides the search for better molecules by directing and learning from each cycle of our high speed, automated wet lab that synthesizes and characterizes hundreds of antibodies each week. We’ll highlight key features of our platform and share several case studies of protein engineering using this novel platform.

*De novo* Design and Machine Learning Guided Optimization of Antibody Therapeutics

![](/images/default-source/speakerphotos/biswas_surge-jpg_ba6f191b-9806-45ab-83e1-0d7f8ba47205.tmb-.jpg?Status=Master&sfvrsn=2f695cb5_1)
Surge Biswas, PhD, Founder & CEO, Nabla Bio, Inc.

![](/images/default-source/speakerphotos/biswas_surge-jpg_ba6f191b-9806-45ab-83e1-0d7f8ba47205.tmb-.jpg?Status=Master&sfvrsn=2f695cb5_1)

We developed a method for de novo antibody design using antigen structure alone. Across multiple antigens, we characterized binding strength for ~105 antibody designs and observed 10s-100s of strong binders. Further, all designs were assayed in multiplex for stability and polyspecificity, and this revealed a broad array of binding and developability trade-offs. These results highlight the importance of integrating machine learning with high-throughput, multi-property measurements for the holistic design of antibody therapeutics.

Peptide Hit Identification and Lead Optimization Using Artificial Intelligence Approaches

![](/images/default-source/speakerphotos/lis_ewa-jpg_77b0487b-1a0b-4d22-8b9c-3aa514d6bf15.tmb-.jpg?Status=Master&sfvrsn=57bb054c_1)
Ewa Lis, PhD, Founder & CTO, Koliber Biosciences

![](/images/default-source/speakerphotos/lis_ewa-jpg_77b0487b-1a0b-4d22-8b9c-3aa514d6bf15.tmb-.jpg?Status=Master&sfvrsn=57bb054c_1)

Successful peptide drug discovery programs today require attainment of multiple performance metrics to progress a compound to clinical stage. To aid decision-making, Koliber has developed an AI peptide platform based on state-of-the-art machine learning methods to analyze peptide properties, profile positions, and predict new variants. The capabilities and wet-lab validation of the AI platform will be demonstrated with examples from immunology and antimicrobial peptide discovery and optimization.

# PLENARY KEYNOTE PROGRAM

# ROOM LOCATION: Constitution A + B

Plenary Chairperson’s Remarks

An-Dinh Nguyen, Team Lead, Discovery on Target, Cambridge Healthtech Institute

PLENARY: Pirating Biology to Detect and Degrade Extracellular Proteins

![](/images/default-source/speakerphotos/wells_james-jpg_2e2d0d21-9aef-476a-b7df-e5d6330c958d.tmb-.jpg?Status=Master&sfvrsn=51c24ac6_0)
James A. Wells, PhD, Professor, Departments of Pharmaceutical Chemistry and Cellular & Molecular Pharmacology, University of California, San Francisco

![](/images/default-source/speakerphotos/wells_james-jpg_2e2d0d21-9aef-476a-b7df-e5d6330c958d.tmb-.jpg?Status=Master&sfvrsn=51c24ac6_0)

In contrast to intracellular PROTACs, approaches to degrade extracellular proteins are just emerging. I’ll describe our recent progress to harness natural mechanisms such as transmembrane E3 ligases to degrade extracellular proteins using fully genetically encoded bispecific antibodies we call AbTACs. We have also engineered a peptide ligase which can be tethered to cells to detect proteolysis events and target them with recombinant antibodies for greater selectivity for the tumor microenvironment.

PLENARY: Therapeutic Modalities for Neuroscience Diseases

![](/images/default-source/speakerphotos/villalobos_anabella-jpg_e64b734c-c302-4581-89f4-d5dd522f43e8.tmb-.jpg?Status=Master&sfvrsn=f0493625_1)
Anabella Villalobos, PhD, Senior Vice President, Biotherapeutics & Medicinal Sciences, Biogen

![](/images/default-source/speakerphotos/villalobos_anabella-jpg_e64b734c-c302-4581-89f4-d5dd522f43e8.tmb-.jpg?Status=Master&sfvrsn=f0493625_1)

Many effective medicines exist to treat neurological diseases, but medical need remains high. We have a unique multi-modality approach to discover novel therapies and our goal is to find the best modality regardless of biological target. With a multi-modality approach, we aim to expand target space, leverage synergies across modalities, and offer options to patients. Opportunities and challenges associated with small molecules, biologics, oligonucleotides, and gene therapy will be discussed.

enriched_points.expanded_content: AI tools like AlphaFold drastically reduce the time taken to predict protein structures, enabling rapid design and optimization of therapeutic proteins, enhancing drug discovery efficiency.
Advancements in AI, particularly through AlphaFold2, achieve accuracy comparable to experimental methods, ensuring reliable predictions for protein interactions critical in therapeutic development.
AI-driven protein prediction has identified new drug targets in oncology, accelerating the discovery of inhibitors for proteins like CDK20, showcasing significant clinical potential.
AI technologies expedite vaccine design by predicting viral protein structures, aiding in rapid responses to emerging diseases, exemplified during the COVID-19 pandemic.
AI's integration into drug discovery processes is evident through successful case studies, where AI-generated compounds lead to rapid identification of viable drug candidates.
